opp.ais_why:
• The September 10 2025 demo (61 minutes) with Prince Bedell, Michelle DiGioia, Adriana Cano and others showcased AI-powered spend analytics, procurement taxonomies and interactive dashboards. The demo was well received with positive feedback on spend miner. But the buyer questions were about dashboards, taxonomy structure and drill-downs, not AI-specific outcomes.
• The Merlin product line lists only Merlin (no ANA, Agentic or Merlin Intake) and Products are limited to iContract, iSave and Merlin. The deal scope is narrow - Spend + CLM at $110K. The buyer pain is described as no centralized dashboard and Excel-based reporting - an operational analytics problem, not an autonomous-AI driver.
• The most recent call - February 27 2026 with Prince Bedell (26 minutes) - is a CLM RFP follow-up, and the CLM RFP is still being released mid-May. AI has been shown but not demanded. The buyer is asking "How can I drill?" rather than "how does AI act?". Recent engagement is focused on RFP inclusion logistics rather than AI value.

sources: SF Next Step 23-25 Jun 2026: Sandra requested total cost estimate and metrics for pricing
SF Products__c: iContract;Merlin (CLM + AI/Merlin in scope, indicating digital transformation and AI appetite)

SF Contact roles: Sandra Hill Senior Category Manager IT & Digital Experience, Prince Bedell Head of Global Procurement CoE (procurement/IT-led, business case likely exists at C-level but not captured)
metrics.narrative: The buyer has articulated a need to modernize CLM (contract lifecycle management) and integrate spend analytics, but no quantified business case has been captured. Sandra Hill requested metrics for pricing on 23-24 Jun 2026, indicating the buyer is building a cost model, but we have not documented the financial or operational pain driving the project (e.g., contract leakage rate, manual workload hours, compliance risk cost, expected ROI). The Next Step history and contact-role titles (Senior Category Manager IT, Head of Procurement CoE) suggest a procurement-efficiency and digital-transformation initiative, likely tied to enterprise spend visibility and compliance, but without a recorded compelling event, savings target, or executive-sponsored business case. The risk is that price becomes the primary decision criterion in the absence of a co-developed quantified value narrative. We must surface and document the CFO- or Chief Supply Chain Officer-level business case (cost avoidance, cycle time reduction, compliance improvement) to anchor our differentiation and justify our price position going into Round 2.

SF Next Step 23-25 Jun 2026: Sandra Hill requested cost estimate, received metrics, quote forwarded (Sandra now operational lead, Prince absent)
SF OpportunityContactRole: Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager IT & Digital Experience), operational eval lead since June, no prior relationship or advocacy evidence
champion.narrative: Prince Bedell (Head of Global Procurement CoE) was the initial strategic champion and entry point, but his engagement has cooled since mid-May 2026, and operational ownership has shifted to Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager IT & Digital Experience), with whom we have no established relationship or evidence of advocacy. Prince's champion activity: he responded after numerous outreach attempts (25 Mar), confirmed Zycus would be included in the RFP vendor list (24 Apr), attended the Gartner event in Orlando and received an iManage demo from Ronit (14 May), and was the primary contact through April-May. However, the Next Step log shows no direct Prince engagement after 14 May; all subsequent activity (RFI submission 10 Jun, cost estimate request 23-24 Jun) flows through Sandra. Prince has not introduced us to the Economic Buyer, provided competitive intel, or driven the RFP timeline on our behalf. Sandra Hill emerged as the operational lead in June (she requested metrics and the cost estimate), but there is no evidence she is advocating for Zycus; her role appears to be neutral project manager/gatekeeper. We have no confirmed champion with active advocacy, executive access, or willingness to coach us on the competitive landscape. The risk is that we are one of several vendors in a price-driven bake-off, with no internal voice pushing our differentiation or navigating us through the decision process. The move is to re-engage Prince within the next 7 days (via his manager Alexa Bradley if needed, framed as executive alignment) to understand why his engagement dropped, confirm his continued sponsorship, and secure an introduction to the Economic Buyer. In parallel, build rapport with Sandra by providing high-value technical content (e.g., Merlin AI use cases, ROI model) to establish her as a coach.

No other Salesforce fields or tasks mentioning competitors
competition.narrative: The competitive field is completely unknown. The Salesforce Competitors__c field is empty, and the Next Step log, contact-role notes, and all other fields contain no mention of competing vendors. The 23 Jun Next Step states 'RFP to be issued to shortlisted vendors, exact date not provided,' confirming a multi-vendor competition, but the names, positioning, and our relative standing from Round 1 RFI are not captured. We are operating blind into Round 2. The likely competitors for an enterprise CLM+Spend deal of this profile are Coupa (strong in spend, weak in CLM), Ivalua (spend+procurement suite), Icertis (CLM leader, weak in spend analytics), SAP Ariba (incumbent risk if Norwegian uses SAP), and specialist CLM players (Agiloft, Ironclad). If Norwegian has an incumbent CLM or ERP system, that vendor may have an integration/services advantage. If price is a primary criterion (per Sandra's 23 Jun cost-estimate request), we may face a lower-cost disruptor. Our wedge (Merlin AI for negotiation automation and spend enrichment) is differentiated IF the buyer values AI and IF we can demonstrate ROI, but without knowing the competitive narrative we cannot position it effectively. The risk is high: we may lose on an unknown competitor strength (price, incumbent status, services breadth, faster implementation) without the chance to counter. The move is to ask Prince or Sandra directly who else is in the RFP and what our Round 1 RFI feedback/score was, framed as ensuring we address any gaps in Round 2. If they will not disclose, we must infer from public signals (LinkedIn, their tech stack, analyst reports) and position Merlin as the high-value, hard-to-replicate differentiator.

sources: SF Products__c: iContract;Merlin (CLM + AI/Merlin in scope, implies pain around contract management and spend analytics)
SF Contact roles: Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager IT & Digital Experience), Prince Bedell (Head of Global Procurement CoE) (operational pain owners, procurement/IT efficiency)
SF Next Step 2 Mar 2026: CLM RFP already underway (urgency signal, but pain not quantified)
SF Next Step 23 Jun 2026: cost estimate requested (budget-conscious, but business case not documented)
identify_pain.narrative: The pain is operational inefficiency and lack of visibility in contract management and spend analytics, but it has not been quantified or anchored to a named pain owner at the executive level. The product scope (iContract + Merlin per SF Products__c) and the buyer organization (procurement CoE + IT, per contact roles) indicate that Norwegian is struggling with manual CLM processes, fragmented contract data, and insufficient spend intelligence, likely across a complex, multi-brand cruise operation (NCL, Oceania, Regent). The Next Step log references a 'CLM RFP already underway' as of March 2026 and a formal RFI/RFP process, signaling urgency and executive mandate, but the specific pain points (e.g., contract leakage, compliance risk, supplier consolidation opportunity, savings target) have not been documented. Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager IT & Digital Experience) and Prince Bedell (Head of Procurement CoE) are the operational pain owners, but the strategic pain owner (Chief Supply Chain Officer, CFO, or Chief Digital Officer) has not been identified or engaged. The 23 Jun request for a cost estimate suggests budget sensitivity, which may indicate that the pain is significant enough to justify investment but not yet framed as a burning platform. The risk is that without a quantified, executive-sponsored pain narrative, the decision defaults to lowest cost rather than highest value. We need to surface and document the C-level pain (cost, risk, growth enablement) and co-develop the business case with the Economic Buyer.

sources: SF Next Step March-June 2026: no mention of MSA, SOW, legal, InfoSec, or contract process
SF OpportunityContactRole: no Legal, InfoSec, or Compliance contact roles
No Avoma calls discussing paper process
SF CloseDate 19 Nov 2026, 141 days out, with no contracting milestones defined
paper_process.narrative: The contracting, legal, and InfoSec process is completely unknown. No discussion of MSA, SOW, redlines, InfoSec questionnaire, data privacy review, or approval timeline has been recorded in the Next Step log or any Salesforce field. Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings is a publicly traded company operating globally; a SaaS/AI procurement of this scope will require legal review, InfoSec clearance (especially for contract data and AI models), and likely procurement/compliance sign-offs. The absence of any paper-process visibility with 141 days to close and no Economic Buyer engagement creates late-stage risk: if InfoSec or legal review surfaces a blocker (data residency, AI explainability, liability caps, indemnification), it could compress or break the 19 Nov close date. The risk is amplified because we are still in Round 1 RFI/quote stage; if the contracting process is not scoped until after vendor selection, we may have only 30-60 days for legal/InfoSec/procurement, which is tight for an enterprise deal. The move is to ask Sandra or Prince (or, better, the Economic Buyer once identified) to outline the post-selection contracting process, InfoSec requirements, and approval gates as part of the Round 2 RFP kickoff, so we can front-load any known friction points (e.g., pre-clear our AI data-handling terms, align on liability language).

sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager IT & Digital Experience), Prince Bedell (Head of Global Procurement CoE); no Economic Buyer, Executive Sponsor, or C-level contact role present
SF Next Step 23 Jun 2026: Sandra requested cost estimate to be factored as additional evaluation criteria (implies higher-level cost comparison, but no named approver)

economic_buyer.narrative: The Economic Buyer who will authorize the $110k+ investment and sign the contract has not been identified or engaged. All recorded interactions are with Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager) and Prince Bedell (Head of Procurement CoE), both mid-level operational recommenders without budget authority for a six-figure enterprise software deal. Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd is a publicly traded, multi-billion-dollar cruise operator; a CLM+Spend+AI transformation of this scope would require C-level approval, most likely the Chief Supply Chain Officer or CFO. The Next Step log (March-June 2026) contains no reference to executive briefings, sponsor engagement, or budget-holder meetings. Prince confirmed Zycus would be included in the RFP (24 Apr) and attended the Gartner event (14 May), indicating operational sponsorship, but he has not introduced us to the economic decision-maker or validated budget. Sandra's 23 Jun request for a cost estimate to be factored as 'additional evaluation criteria' signals that price will be compared across vendors at a higher level, yet we have no line of sight to who makes that final call or what their priorities are. This is a critical gap: without Economic Buyer engagement before the shortlist decision, we risk late-stage rejection or a price-driven decision that ignores our differentiation. The highest-leverage move is to ask Prince or Sandra to facilitate an executive briefing with the Chief Supply Chain Officer or CFO within the next 14 days, framed as aligning on strategic objectives and ROI before Round 2.

sources: SF Next Step 10 Jun 2026: Round 1 RFI submitted, shortlisted vendors will be invited to upcoming RFP
SF Next Step 23 Jun 2026: RFP to be issued to shortlisted vendors, exact date not provided
SF Next Step 24 Apr 2026: Prince confirmed CLM RFP release mid-May, Zycus included
SF Contact roles: Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager IT & Digital Experience, operational eval lead), Prince Bedell (Head of Global Procurement CoE, strategic sponsor)
SF CloseDate 19 Nov 2026, 141 days out; no contract/legal/approval milestones recorded
decision_process.narrative: The decision process is a formal, multi-stage procurement RFP led by the procurement and IT organizations, but key milestones and the approval chain remain unclear. The sequence from the Next Step log: (1) Zycus included in vendor list (24 Apr 2026), (2) Round 1 RFI issued and submitted (reviewed 4 Jun, submitted 10 Jun), (3) shortlist decision pending (date not provided per 23 Jun), (4) Round 2 RFP to shortlisted vendors (date not provided), (5) cost estimates requested from vendors for comparison (23 Jun), (6) final vendor selection, (7) contracting and close (target 19 Nov 2026). The operational eval is managed by Sandra Hill (Senior Category Manager IT) and supported by Prince Bedell (Head of Procurement CoE), but the approval authority and executive gate are unknown. The Next Step entry 'exact date not provided' for the shortlist decision and Round 2 RFP indicates the buyer has not committed to a timeline, creating slip risk. No mention of legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, MSA/SOW process, or CFO/C-level sign-off gates. The call sequence (no Avoma calls discovered) offers no additional visibility. The risk is a compressed or opaque back-end process: if shortlisting drags into August or the final approval requires surprise executive buy-in, the 19 Nov close is at risk. We need Sandra or Prince to confirm the shortlist decision date, the Round 2 RFP timeline, and the internal approval chain (who signs, what gates) within the next 7 days.

sources: SF Next Step 10 Jun 2026: Round 1 RFI submitted, shortlisted vendors will be invited to upcoming RFP
SF Next Step 23 Jun 2026: Sandra requested total cost estimate to be factored as additional evaluation criteria (price is a criterion)
SF Products__c: iContract;Merlin (CLM + AI in scope)
SF Next Step 4 Jun 2026: Final review of CLM RFI before submission (implies structured RFI with defined questions/criteria, but content not captured)
decision_criteria.narrative: The decision criteria are partially visible through the RFI/RFP structure and product scope, but not yet explicitly documented from the buyer. Norwegian issued a formal Round 1 RFI (submitted by Zycus 10 Jun 2026) and will issue a Round 2 RFP to shortlisted vendors (date not provided per 23 Jun Next Step), indicating a structured, multi-stage evaluation with defined functional and technical requirements. The product scope (iContract + Merlin per SF Products__c) confirms that CLM functionality and AI/Merlin capabilities are in scope, suggesting criteria around contract authoring/approval workflow, AI-driven negotiation or analytics, and integration with their spend data. Sandra Hill's 23 Jun request for a total cost estimate 'to be factored as additional evaluation criteria' explicitly adds price/TCO as a decision factor. However, we have not captured the weighted scoring model, the specific use cases or test scenarios the RFP will require, or how AI/Merlin differentiates us in their rubric. The Next Step log contains no record of a BRD review, shoe-fit session, or criteria walkthrough. The risk is that we are competing blind: if the criteria emphasize lowest cost or a capability where a competitor is stronger (e.g., incumbent integration, services breadth), we may lose without knowing why. Before Round 2, we must obtain the RFP scoring framework and criteria from Sandra or Prince, and position Merlin's AI negotiation and spend enrichment as high-value, hard-to-replicate differentiators that justify a premium.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit signal is ambiguous. Merlin is in scope per Products__c, suggesting the buyer sees value in AI for CLM and spend analytics. Ronit demoed iManage (procurement AI) to Prince at Gartner Orlando 14 May 2026, and Prince showed interest enough to attend, indicating curiosity. However, no AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, or AIS_Why__c recorded, and no Avoma calls to assess whether the buyer articulated AI use cases, pain points that AI solves, or AI ROI expectations. Sandra's 23 Jun focus on cost estimate rather than AI differentiation may signal price-over-innovation priority. Without deeper evidence, the tier is AI Curious (in scope, some interest, but not yet a strategic priority or burning platform).

champion_strength.summary: Champion strength is weak and at risk. Prince Bedell was the initial strategic sponsor (confirmed Zycus in RFP, attended Gartner event, provided operational access), but his engagement cooled after mid-May with no recorded contact since 14 May 2026. Operational ownership shifted to Sandra Hill in June; she is managing the RFI/quote process but shows no evidence of advocacy (no competitive coaching, no executive introduction, no internal navigation). We have no active champion with Economic Buyer access or willingness to push our differentiation. Risk is high: without a champion we are one of several vendors in a price-driven, arms-length procurement bake-off.

items.action: Executive connect: the deal owner's manager facilitates briefing with the relevant stakeholder Chain Officer or CFO to align on strategic objectives, validate budget, and co-develop quantified ROI business case (cost avoidance, cycle time, compliance improvement) before shortlist decision. Frame as ensuring executive alignment and maximizing value realization, not as sales pressure. Goal: identify and engage Economic Buyer, surface C-level priorities, and anchor our differentiation at the decision-making layer.
items.trigger: Economic Buyer not identified after 4 months and $110k+ deal with mid-level contacts only; Sandra and Prince cannot authorize contract; shortlist decision imminent with no C-level sponsorship.
items.expected_effect: Engages Economic Buyer, de-risks late-stage approval stall, elevates conversation from price to strategic value, increases win probability by 25-30 points if EB sponsorship secured.

items.action: Deal team: re-engage Prince Bedell directly (call or in-person if in Miami/South Florida area) to understand why his engagement dropped after May, confirm his continued strategic sponsorship, ask for competitive intel (who else is in the RFP, our Round 1 RFI score/feedback), and request introduction to the relevant stakeholder Officer or Economic Buyer. If Prince is unresponsive, escalate through his LinkedIn/network or ask Alexa Bradley (deal owner's manager) to reach his peer.
items.trigger: Prince Bedell (initial strategic champion, Head of Procurement CoE) last engaged 14 May 2026; operational ownership shifted to Sandra in June; no executive access or competitive coaching since.
items.expected_effect: Revives champion relationship, surfaces competitive intel and RFI feedback, reopens executive introduction path; if unsuccessful, confirms champion is lost and we must build Sandra or find new sponsor.

items.action: Deal team: make Merlin AI negotiation automation and spend enrichment the centerpiece of Round 2 RFP response. Deliver a quantified ROI model showing contract cycle time reduction, supplier price optimization, and compliance improvement (with named CPG/travel customer proof points). Include a live Merlin demo offer. Ask Sandra directly how AI capabilities will be weighted in vendor scoring and what use cases the RFP will test. Position Merlin as the high-value, hard-to-replicate differentiator that justifies our price vs lower-cost competitors.
items.trigger: Merlin in scope but AI value narrative not established; Sandra's 23 Jun cost-estimate request signals price sensitivity; competitive field unknown; risk of price-driven decision if differentiation not anchored.
items.expected_effect: Shifts evaluation focus from price to innovation and ROI, establishes Merlin as a category-of-one capability, increases defensibility against lower-cost competitors, and surfaces buyer's AI appetite and criteria weighting.

items.action: Deal team: request from Sandra or Prince (a) the shortlist decision date, (b) the Round 2 RFP timeline and milestones, (c) the post-selection contracting process (MSA/SOW, legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, approval gates), and (d) our Round 1 RFI score and feedback. Frame as ensuring we allocate the right resources and address any gaps proactively in Round 2. Use the information to build a realistic close plan and front-load known friction points (e.g., pre-clear AI data-handling terms, align on liability caps).
items.trigger: Shortlist decision and RFP timeline 'exact date not provided' per 23 Jun Next Step; contract/legal/InfoSec process completely unknown; 141 days to close with no milestone visibility.
items.expected_effect: Creates milestone visibility, de-risks slip from compressed finalist/contract phases, surfaces any blockers (InfoSec, legal, approval chain) early, and allows proactive planning.

items.action: Deal team: build relationship with Sandra Hill by providing high-value, consultative content before Round 2 RFP: (a) Merlin AI use-case playbook for cruise/hospitality procurement, (b) ROI model template she can use internally, (c) named customer references (CPG or travel/hospitality) willing to speak to CLM+AI value. Goal: shift Sandra from neutral gatekeeper to coach by demonstrating thought leadership and making her job easier. Ask her for feedback on what would make Zycus's Round 2 response most compelling to the eval committee.
items.trigger: Sandra Hill is operational eval lead since June but shows no advocacy; no established relationship; she requested cost estimate 23 Jun, signaling price focus; need to build rapport and trust to gain coaching access.
items.expected_effect: Converts Sandra from neutral project manager to advocate or at least coach; surfaces internal eval criteria and competitive intel; increases likelihood she champions Zycus in internal discussions.

confidence_signals.summary: Confidence signals are mixed. Positive: formal RFI process with Zycus included (24 Apr), Round 1 RFI submitted (10 Jun), initial quote delivered (25 Jun), LastActivityDate 29 Jun (2 days ago), products in scope (iContract + Merlin), and rep engagement is current. Negative: no two-way buyer calls or verified buyer engagement recorded (all movement is rep-driven task updates), champion (Prince) engagement cooling since mid-May, operational shift to Sandra (no advocacy evidence), Economic Buyer unmapped, competitive field unknown, shortlist/RFP timeline unconfirmed, and no quantified business case. The 25 Jun rep outreach (quote forwarded) is awaiting buyer reply; lack of response in 6 days is a minor cooling signal but not yet critical. Overall: deal is procedurally on track (RFI submitted, quote in) but strategically fragile (no executive alignment, no champion advocacy, blind to competition).

north_star_verdict.math: Created 27 Feb 2026, 124 days old, in Formal Evaluation since at least early March. Close date 19 Nov 2026 is 141 days out. Deal has spent ~120+ days in Formal Evaluation (RFI process March-June). Round 1 RFI submitted 10 Jun, initial quote forwarded 25 Jun per Next Step. Stage velocity is reasonable for an enterprise CLM RFP cycle. However, no confirmed shortlist decision date, no Round 2 RFP timeline, and 141 days to close with Economic Buyer and Decision Maker still unmapped creates execution risk. Required pace: shortlist confirmation within 14 days, Round 2 RFP/demos through August, vendor selection by mid-September, commercials/contract September-October to hit 19 Nov close. Pace is achievable if shortlist is confirmed soon and we advance, but slip risk is moderate given milestone visibility gaps and procurement-layer engagement only.

Pipeline forecast with 141 days to close, but Economic Buyer unmapped, Decision Maker unidentified, no two-way executive engagement, competitive field unknown, and shortlist/RFP timeline unconfirmed; forecast is not defensible and should be Omitted until EB engaged and shortlist confirmed
north_star_verdict.headline: RFI submitted, quote delivered, awaiting Round 2 RFP invite; champion cooling, Economic Buyer unmapped, competitive field blind, shortlist date unknown.

flags: Map and engage the Economic Buyer now (likely the CSO per Prince's 27 Feb mention, possibly CFO for $110k approval) before shortlist decision. Request executive briefing with Sandra and Prince to validate strategic fit, build C-level sponsorship, and prevent late-stage approval surprise when contract hits the exec's desk. EB unmapped at mid-RFP (post-RFI, pricing requested) is a red flag — close it before finalist round.

Request competitive intelligence immediately after shortlist decision (expected within 7-14 days): ask Sandra and Prince explicitly who the finalists are, how many vendors, and how we scored on Round 1 RFI. Operating blind into Round 2 RFP risks losing on an unknown competitor strength (price, integration, references) we could address. Use the debrief to tailor Round 2 emphasis on Merlin AI contract intelligence and suite integration as differentiators.

Re-engage Prince Bedell (champion, Head of Procurement CoE, executive access to CSO) within 7 days with a strategic check-in on transformation progress and CLM roadmap fit, before shortlist/finalist decisions. His advocacy brought us into the RFP and he controls executive access, but engagement has cooled since mid-May as operational eval shifted to the relevant stakeholder the champion thread warm and reactivate his advocacy for the final gate — his voice with the CSO will matter at contract approval.
Decision Maker not identified; no contact role or engagement with final approver

best_practice_check.summary: Seven critical gaps block progression: Economic Buyer unmapped, Decision Maker unidentified, competitive field unknown, champion (Prince) cooling, quantified business case missing, shortlist/RFP timeline unconfirmed, contract process unknown. Deal is in formal RFP with quote delivered, but still single-threaded through mid-level procurement/IT contacts with no executive sponsorship or two-way call engagement.

competitive_position.summary: Competitive field is completely unknown. Round 1 RFI submitted 10 Jun 2026, initial quote delivered 25 Jun 2026. Shortlisted vendors will receive Round 2 RFP (date not confirmed). Operating blind: no competitor names, no Round 1 scoring/feedback, no visibility into relative positioning. Sandra's 23 Jun request for cost estimate to be factored as 'additional evaluation criteria' signals price sensitivity and multi-vendor cost comparison. Risk of losing on unknown competitor strength (price, incumbent integration, services) without chance to counter. Wedge is Merlin AI for negotiation automation and spend enrichment, differentiated if buyer values AI and ROI, but competitive narrative not established.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is moderate. Merlin is in scope per Products__c, and Ronit demoed iManage (procurement-intelligence AI use case) to Prince at the Gartner event 14 May 2026, indicating some AI appetite and awareness. However, no follow-up on the Merlin/AI narrative is recorded, no AI-specific business case or ROI model has been co-developed, and no discussion of AI as a differentiator in the RFI or quote process. The risk is that Merlin is listed as a product line item without a compelling, quantified AI value story, allowing competitors to dismiss it as a 'nice to have' or to match with their own AI claims. With no AIS score/status recorded and no Avoma calls to gauge buyer AI appetite, positioning strength is uncertain. The move is to make Merlin negotiation automation and spend enrichment the centerpiece of the Round 2 RFP response, backed by a quantified ROI model and a live demo, and to ask Sandra or Prince how AI capabilities will be weighted in the vendor selection.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Limited visibility into buyer's detailed expectations. Product scope (iContract + Merlin) aligns with CLM and AI/Merlin appetite. Round 1 RFI submitted, but shoe-fit, BRD alignment, and demo feedback not documented. Price sensitivity evident (cost estimate requested 23 Jun). Risk: competing blind without criteria weighting or feedback.

hard.ais_why:
• The September 10 2025 demo (61 minutes) with Prince Bedell, Michelle DiGioia, Adriana Cano and others showcased AI-powered spend analytics, procurement taxonomies and interactive dashboards. The demo was well received with positive feedback on spend miner. But the buyer questions were about dashboards, taxonomy structure and drill-downs, not AI-specific outcomes.
• The Merlin product line lists only Merlin (no ANA, Agentic or Merlin Intake) and Products are limited to iContract, iSave and Merlin. The deal scope is narrow - Spend + CLM at $110K. The buyer pain is described as no centralized dashboard and Excel-based reporting - an operational analytics problem, not an autonomous-AI driver.
• The most recent call - February 27 2026 with Prince Bedell (26 minutes) - is a CLM RFP follow-up, and the CLM RFP is still being released mid-May. AI has been shown but not demanded. The buyer is asking "How can I drill?" rather than "how does AI act?". Recent engagement is focused on RFP inclusion logistics rather than AI value.

Use the debrief to tailor Round 2 emphasis on Merlin AI contract intelligence and suite integration as differentiators.

value.flag: Re-engage Prince Bedell (champion, Head of Procurement CoE, executive access to CSO) within 7 days with a strategic check-in on transformation progress and CLM roadmap fit, before shortlist/finalist decisions. His advocacy brought us into the RFP and he controls executive access, but engagement has cooled since mid-May as operational eval shifted to the relevant stakeholder the champion thread warm and reactivate his advocacy for the final gate — his voice with the CSO will matter at contract approval.
